chain_of_thought: Top native frame below sanitizers/allocators is qri1II, but symbol not resolved in lib; next is DecodeQR in libQREngine/libbcr.

In libQREngine, DecodeQR@0x211100 is a large QR decoding routine that operates on a context derived from param_1/param_2; it performs explicit bounds checks on input dimensions (iVar15, iVar4) and sets up internal buffers accordingly.

The crash site is not visible, but there is no immediate unchecked memcpy/memmove in the decompiled snippet; it mostly manipulates offsets into a single allocated work buffer lVar20 based on validated widths/heights and fixed constants.

The fuzz harness entry is fuzz_one_input; the JNI bridge Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decode is not present in the analyzed lib exports, suggesting either stripped/obfuscated symbol or different lib version; however, stack shows qri1II -> DecodeQR -> JNI -> fuzz harness.

Given missing symbol qri1II and JNI entry, I cannot trace exact arguments or see a clear memory corruption. The decompiled QR routine has internal validation and uses constants; the termination is a generic Process Termination: qri1II, not a clear UAF/overflow signal.

Fuzzer may be invoking QR decode with unrealistic/null structures (e.g., invalid param_1 layout) that production Java would never pass, leading to undefined behavior or abort, but without direct evidence of attacker-controlled lengths causing OOB.

No evidence that untrusted data from Java is passed unvalidated into dangerous operations in this snippet; path realism is unclear due to missing Java side (QREngine) in current Jadx project (only BCREngine JNI exports are seen).

Given this, I cannot confidently assert a genuine exploitable bug; more likely this is an instrumentation/harness mismatch or an unmodelled precondition violation rather than a robust, attacker-triggerable vulnerability.
reasons: Crash path involves unresolved qri1II and JNI function, preventing full data-flow tracing from input to crash
Decompiled DecodeQR implementation shows input dimension checks and structured buffer layout, not obviously unsafe memcpy/memmove based on untrusted sizes
Likely harness-only misuse of native API (e.g., invalid param_1 structure) rather than realistic app path from Java with attacker-controlled values

evidence.note: QR decode routine validates width/height and uses internal work buffer; no clear unsafe memcpy tied directly to untrusted size visible.
Trampoline/wrapper to function pointer; actual implementation external or in another region; crash may occur there but not analyzable here.

recommendations: Obtain symbols and full decompilation for qri1II and Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decode to pinpoint actual crash and validate pointer/length handling.
Reproduce crash under realistic Java inputs (e.g., images from app workflows) instead of raw fuzzer harness to check if production code can hit the same failure.
Instrument native QR decoding code with sanitizers (ASan/UBSan) and add argument validations at JNI boundary to guard against malformed param_1/param_2 structures.
assumptions: The unresolved function qri1II is the terminating function but its body is not accessible in the current binary analysis context.
Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decode follows similar patterns as other exported JNI methods (BCREngine_*) that perform some validation before calling native core.
Fuzzer harness may bypass normal Java-level preconditions and construct invalid native structures not reachable from the real app.
limitations: Unable to decompile qri1II and the specific QREngine JNI bridge, blocking full taint analysis from attacker input to crash site.
Lack of stack registers/memory state at crash makes it impossible to see actual parameter values for param_1/param_2 at time of failure.
No Java-side code for com.intsig.nativelib.QREngine available in current Jadx snapshot, so reachability from untrusted sources is uncertain.
